reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: 'When NOT to use' comparison table is exemplary — concrete alternatives with direct links, far above the average resource page
All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with no YAML tabs or fenced TypeScript violations
'Connecting to a bucket' section pairs the Stacktape config example with a working Lambda handler that reads the injected env var — exactly what a first-timer needs to close the loop
FAQ delivers all 10 Q&A pairs across all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service use-case, and comparison questions)
Decision support is consistently present throughout — every opt-in feature (CDN, CORS, versioning, EventBridge) explains when to enable, when to skip, and the tradeoff
Lifecycle rules section is unusually thorough: all 5 rule types are named and explained, the storage class table is grounded in the source union type exactly
Environment variable table names match the example resource name ('uploads'), so STP_UPLOADS_NAME and STP_UPLOADS_ARN are precise and not generic placeholders
Single <ApiReference definitionName="BucketProps" /> placed at the bottom — correct definitionName, correct count
headersPreset table covers all 6 variants from the SupportedHeaderPreset union type with no omissions
CORS section clearly states when you do NOT need it (server-side-only access) before explaining how to configure it — avoids over-configuring
problems: The gatsby-static-website preset behavior ('Framework-specific caching', 'Gatsby builds') is described in the table but the source JSDoc explicitly groups only astro/sveltekit/nuxt — it describes gatsby only implicitly by inclusion in SupportedHeaderPreset. The prose description for gatsby is an inference, not sourced directly, making it a mild unsupported claim.

The CDN section links to '/resources/networking/cdn' for 'full CDN configuration options' but BucketCdnConfiguration extends CdnConfiguration whose full type is not visible in the provided source — the link is reasonable but the claim about what CdnConfiguration contains (custom domains, WAF, price class) is made without direct source grounding for the Bucket-specific cdn path.

Opening paragraph is 46 words — within the 40-75 range — but the second sentence ('Buckets are the most common way to store unstructured data on AWS') is a broad claim with no source grounding. Replaceable with a concrete capability statement.

The 'Uploading files on deploy' section mentions fileOptions and excludeFilesPatterns in prose but does not show them in a code example. For a first-timer, the headersPreset example is shown while the more powerful fileOptions is left abstract — a small completeness gap for upload customization.
optionalImprovements: Tighten gatsby-static-website description to match the source's grouping: the JSDoc explicitly names only astro/sveltekit/nuxt as 'hashed build assets cached forever, HTML always fresh'. Either lump gatsby into that group's description or note that its specific behavior follows a similar pattern.

Add one concrete CDN tradeoff the target audience cares about: CloudFront cache propagation time (changes take several minutes to invalidate globally). This is the most common surprise for first-timers enabling CDN on a bucket.

The 'Uploading files on deploy' section would benefit from a brief fileOptions snippet showing how to override Cache-Control for a specific glob (e.g., images). The section currently mentions it in prose but it's a commonly needed configuration that the example could make concrete.

Consider adding a note in the 'Event-driven workflows' section about the S3 event trigger configuration side (on the LambdaFunction resource, not the Bucket) — the current text says 'configure S3 triggers on the Lambda function side' and links to the trigger page, which is correct, but a first-timer may not realize the bucket itself needs no trigger property.

The opening sentence 'Buckets are the most common way to store unstructured data on AWS' is a generic claim. Replace with a capability statement: e.g., 'A single bucket can scale to any volume without capacity planning.'

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: All five lifecycle rule types and all five storage classes are documented with accurate comparison tables sourced directly from the union types in buckets.d.ts.
Decision support for CDN, versioning, and event triggers is concrete — each opt-in feature includes when to enable, when the default is fine, and a cost or tradeoff signal.
The CORS section correctly identifies when CORS is not needed (server-side-only access), which saves real debugging time for readers who would otherwise configure it unnecessarily.

Ten FAQ pairs cover all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (env vars, custom domain), AWS use-case (S3 max object size, encryption, cost reduction), and comparison (Bucket vs HostingBucket, S3 vs EFS).
The application code example in 'Connecting to a bucket' is realistic and directly usable, correctly referencing STP_UPLOADS_NAME and STP_UPLOADS_ARN matching the resource name in the config example.
All six headersPreset values from the source SupportedHeaderPreset union are enumerated in the table.
The directoryUpload Warning callout accurately reflects the source JSDoc ('Replaces all existing bucket contents. Don't use for buckets with user-uploaded or application-generated files').
problems: The gatsby-static-website preset is described only as 'Framework-specific caching' — the source JSDoc groups astro/sveltekit/nuxt together with 'hashed build assets cached forever, HTML always fresh' but does not mention gatsby in that grouping. The page's description is a reasonable inference but not source-grounded.

The claim 'Custom corsRules override the permissive defaults' is an inference from the source ('When true with no rules, uses permissive defaults') and is not explicitly stated — acceptable but slightly underdetermined by the source.
optionalImprovements: Split 'Versioning and encryption' into two separate H2 sections. They are independent opt-in properties with different use cases (versioning drives cost growth; encryption is about compliance posture) and warrant separate decision-support context.

Explicitly state that gatsby-static-website follows the same caching strategy as the other framework presets (hashed assets cached forever, HTML always fresh) rather than leaving the description as 'Framework-specific caching', since the source JSDoc does not describe it separately.

The fileOptions property within directoryUpload is only mentioned in one sentence of prose. A minimal code snippet showing how to set a long Cache-Control header on image files while keeping HTML uncached would make this significantly more useful for teams doing fine-grained caching control.

The CDN section could briefly note that CloudFront distribution creation and updates can take several minutes to propagate — a common surprise for first-time users who expect immediate availability after deploy. This is worth a one-liner even without explicit source grounding, as it is a well-known operational reality.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Answer-first opening paragraph is tight, self-contained (52 words), and directly answers 'what is this and why does it matter' — extractable verbatim by AI engines.

Extensive, well-placed tables throughout: access modes, headersPreset presets, lifecycle rule types, storage classes, env vars injected by connectTo. Tables are the highest-citation format and this page uses them consistently.
Every opt-in feature (CDN, CORS, versioning, encryption, EventBridge notifications) has explicit when-to-enable / when-the-default-is-fine / tradeoff prose — not just config recipes.
All five lifecycle rule types from the source union are enumerated and documented with a table, working code example, and individual explanations. Exactly zero variants are silently dropped.

FAQ has exactly 10 questions spanning all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (Q1, Q3, Q4, Q5, Q7), AWS service use-case (Q2, Q6, Q9, Q10), and choosing/comparison (Q3, Q8). Answers are 2–4 sentence paragraphs, extractable as featured snippets.
All Stacktape config examples correctly use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />` with `label: 'TypeScript'`. No hand-authored YAML Stacktape config blocks are present.
Application handler code (S3 SDK usage) is in a plain fenced block, correctly separated from Stacktape config examples.
Single `<ApiReference definitionName="BucketProps" />` placed at a sensible position (after content, before FAQ). The definitionName matches the source type exactly.
The 'When NOT to use' table comparing Bucket to HostingBucket, NextjsWeb, RelationalDatabase, and EfsFilesystem is concise, decision-oriented, and source-grounded.
The connectTo environment variable table uses the example resource name (`uploads`) to produce `STP_UPLOADS_NAME` and `STP_UPLOADS_ARN` — exactly as required by the canonical naming rule.
problems: The 'Versioning and encryption' H2 section combines two structurally unrelated features into a single section. Versioning is about data safety and cost; encryption is about compliance and security. A reader scanning for 'how do I enable encryption' must read past versioning content. No source-driven reason to combine them.

The `directoryUpload.fileOptions` property is only acknowledged in one sentence ('For more control, use `fileOptions`...') with no example and no explanation of the filter structure (includePattern, excludePattern, headers, tags). Readers deploying real frontends will commonly need this.

The `beforeBucketSync` / `afterBucketSync` lifecycle hooks in the source (`__helpers.d.ts`) are directly relevant to the `directoryUpload` workflow (e.g., running a build before sync) but are not mentioned or linked to in the 'Uploading files on deploy' section.

The CDN section's code example shows only `rewriteRoutesForSinglePageApp: true` without explaining that this value controls whether extensionless paths (e.g., `/about`) are routed to `index.html`. Prose does explain it, but the explanation comes after the example rather than anchoring the value before it.

The CORS example code block has a trailing newline inside the template literal (blank line before the closing backtick), which is a minor formatting inconsistency but could affect copy-paste behavior depending on the renderer.
optionalImprovements: Split 'Versioning and encryption' into two separate H2 sections ('Versioning' and 'Encryption') so readers scanning the TOC can find each independently.

Add a compact example to the 'Uploading files on deploy' section showing `fileOptions` with an `includePattern` and a `Cache-Control` header override, since this is the primary way to customize caching beyond the headersPreset.
Add a one-line mention of `beforeBucketSync` / `afterBucketSync` hooks in the directoryUpload section with a link to the hooks docs, since 'build before upload' is the most common real-world pattern.
Move the CDN `rewriteRoutesForSinglePageApp: true` explanation to appear before the code example (or inline it as a comment) so the property value is anchored before the reader sees it in code.
Remove the trailing blank line inside the CORS example code field before the closing backtick to prevent any renderer artifacts.

The CDN section could briefly enumerate which `CdnConfiguration` properties are inherited (e.g., custom domains, WAF, price class) rather than relying entirely on the external link — even a one-line enumeration would help readers know what's available without navigating away.

Consider adding a note in the 'Uploading files on deploy' section that `directoryUpload` triggers `beforeBucketSync` / `afterBucketSync` hooks, not `beforeDeploy` / `afterDeploy`, which is a common gotcha for teams that want to run a build step before upload.

verifierResults.summary: The S3 Bucket draft page is well-written and overwhelmingly accurate. Property names, types, defaults, union values, lifecycle rule types, storage classes, access modes, CORS behavior, and referenceable parameters all match the source files. Code examples use correct class names, import patterns, and CodeBlock conventions. Cross-links all resolve to valid routes. The FAQ has exactly 10 questions with good category coverage. I found only minor issues — two embellished details not explicitly supported by the source, and one inconsistency in the headersPreset table.

issues.statement: URL normalization is enabled by default and appends `index.html` to directory-style paths for clean URLs when serving HTML files.
issues.reason: The source describes URL normalization as 'useful for serving HTML files from the bucket with clean URLs' but does not specify the mechanism of appending 'index.html'. The 'appends index.html' detail is inferred behavior not stated in the source type definition.
issues.suggestedFix: Rephrase to match the source wording: 'URL normalization is enabled by default for clean URLs when serving HTML files from the bucket. Disable it with `disableUrlNormalization: true` if your bucket paths don't follow directory-style conventions.'

evidence.quote: URL normalization is enabled by default and is useful for serving HTML files from the bucket with clean URLs.

issues.statement: Stacktape automatically grants IAM permissions for reading, writing, and deleting objects, and injects environment variables with the bucket's name and ARN.
issues.reason: The source's connectTo documentation mentions 'S3 read/write' as an example in ResourceAccessProps and lists bucket env vars as NAME and ARN, but does not explicitly confirm 'deleting' as a granted permission. The 'deleting' claim is likely correct but not explicitly stated in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Soften to 'Stacktape automatically grants IAM permissions and injects environment variables with the bucket's name and ARN.' or verify the exact permissions from the connectTo resolver code.

evidence.quote: Grants IAM permissions (e.g., S3 read/write, SQS send/receive)

issues.statement: The headersPreset table gives `astro-static-website` the description 'Hashed build assets cached forever, HTML always fresh' while `sveltekit-static-website` and `nuxt-static-website` only get 'Framework-specific caching'.
issues.reason: The source groups all three framework presets (astro, sveltekit, nuxt) together with the same description: 'Framework-specific caching (hashed build assets cached forever, HTML always fresh).' The draft gives the detailed description only to Astro while the other two get the generic label, creating an inconsistency with the source.
issues.suggestedFix: Either give all three framework presets the same detailed description ('Hashed build assets cached forever, HTML always fresh') or use the generic 'Framework-specific caching' for all three consistently.

evidence.quote: **`astro-static-website`** / **`sveltekit-static-website`** / **`nuxt-static-website`**: Framework-specific caching (hashed build assets cached forever, HTML always fresh).

positiveFindings: All BucketProps property names (directoryUpload, accessibility, cors, versioning, encryption, lifecycleRules, enableEventBusNotifications, cdn) exactly match the source type definitions.

All five lifecycle rule types (expiration, non-current-version-expiration, class-transition, non-current-version-class-transition, abort-incomplete-multipart-upload) are correctly documented with matching property names (daysAfterUpload, daysAfterVersioned, daysAfterInitiation, storageClass).
All five storage class values (STANDARD_IA, ONEZONE_IA, INTELLIGENT_TIERING, GLACIER, DEEP_ARCHIVE) match the source union type exactly.
All three accessibility modes (private, public-read, public-read-write) with correct default (private) match the source.
The six headersPreset values all match the SupportedHeaderPreset union type in the source.
The enableEventBusNotifications default of false is correctly stated, matching the @default false annotation in the source.
BucketReferencableParam ('name' | 'arn') is correctly reflected in the connectTo environment variables table (STP_UPLOADS_NAME, STP_UPLOADS_ARN).
The ApiReference definitionName 'BucketProps' correctly matches the source interface name.
CORS allowedMethods values (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD, *) exactly match the BucketCorsRule union type.
All cross-links resolve to valid routes in the documentation navigation index.

Code examples consistently use correct CodeBlock conventions with intellisense, TypeScript label, defineConfig, and proper Stacktape class names (Bucket, LambdaFunction, StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging).
The 'When NOT to use' section is correctly an H2 peer section, not nested under 'When to use'.
The FAQ has exactly 10 questions spanning all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service use-case, choosing/comparison).
The disableS3TransferAcceleration default (false, meaning acceleration is enabled by default) is correctly stated per the source @default annotation.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the provided Bucket type source, uses the required TypeScript CodeBlock pattern, includes exactly one ApiReference with the correct Props name, and avoids hand-authored Stacktape YAML. I found several source-grounding issues where the page overstates Bucket behavior or uses unsupported property paths/permissions wording.

issues.statement: "Enable the `cdn` property on your bucket and configure the domain through the CDN settings."
issues.reason: The provided Bucket CDN type only shows `BucketCdnConfiguration extends CdnConfiguration` plus bucket-specific CDN options. The provided source does not include the `CdnConfiguration` shape or any `domain`/custom-domain property under `bucket.cdn`, so this Stacktape-specific configuration path is not grounded in the supplied source.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the FAQ answer to: "A bucket can be placed behind a CDN with the `cdn` property. Custom domain support is documented on the CDN/custom domains pages; configure it using the CDN options shown there." Avoid saying the domain is configured through bucket CDN settings unless the `CdnConfiguration` source is provided.

evidence.quote: cdn?: BucketCdnConfiguration;

issues.statement: "Stacktape automatically grants IAM permissions for reading, writing, and deleting objects" via `connectTo`, and "Use `connectTo` to grant your compute resources access — Stacktape handles IAM permissions automatically."
issues.reason: The source grounds read/write access and automatic IAM permission grants for Bucket via `connectTo`, but it does not state delete permission. The general `connectTo` source says Bucket gives read/write/delete objects, while the BucketAccessibility source says read/write. Because the page specifically says delete, it should cite the source-grounded wording from `ResourceAccessProps` or avoid expanding beyond read/write where using Bucket source prose.
issues.suggestedFix: Either keep the delete claim only in the `connectTo` section and phrase it exactly as `connectTo` grants Bucket read/write/delete object permissions, or use the narrower BucketAccessibility wording elsewhere: read and write access for resources with IAM permissions.

evidence.quote: **`Bucket`** — read/write/delete objects → `NAME`, `ARN`
- `private` (default): Only compute resources and entities with sufficient IAM permissions can read from or write to the bucket.

issues.statement: "URL normalization is enabled by default and appends `index.html` to directory-style paths for clean URLs when serving HTML files."
issues.reason: The source confirms URL normalization is enabled by default and is useful for clean URLs, but it does not define the specific rewrite behavior as appending `index.html`. That is a Stacktape-specific behavior claim and should be narrowed unless another source provides the implementation detail.
issues.suggestedFix: Write: "URL normalization is enabled by default and is useful for serving HTML files from the bucket with clean URLs. Disable it with `disableUrlNormalization: true` if that behavior does not match your object key layout."

evidence.quote:
URL normalization is enabled by default and is useful for serving HTML files from the bucket with clean URLs.
   *
   * @default false
   */
  disableUrlNormalization?: boolean;

issues.statement: "The first matching rule applies for each preflight request."
issues.reason: The source says first matching rule wins, but only at the level of custom CORS rules. It does not explicitly limit this to preflight requests. That wording may be true for CORS mechanics generally, but as a Stacktape configuration behavior it is more precise to reuse the source wording.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: "For custom CORS rules, the first matching rule wins."

evidence.quote: Custom CORS rules. First matching rule wins for each preflight request.

issues.statement: The `accessPolicyStatements` discussion says: "For fine-grained control beyond the three modes, use `accessPolicyStatements` to attach custom IAM policy statements to the bucket policy."
issues.reason: `accessPolicyStatements` is not a top-level `BucketProps` property. It is nested under `accessibility`, so the page risks sending readers to the wrong property path.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the exact path: "For fine-grained control beyond the three modes, use `accessibility.accessPolicyStatements` to add custom IAM policy statements."

evidence.quote: accessPolicyStatements?: BucketPolicyIamRoleStatement[];

issues.statement: "S3 event triggers invoke a Lambda function directly when objects are created, deleted, or restored."
issues.reason: The S3 integration source supports many event types beyond created, deleted, and restored, including reduced redundancy lost object and replication events. The page later says created/deleted/restored in the FAQ too. This is acceptable as an 80-percent summary only if it does not imply those are the only supported events.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a qualifier: "Common S3 event triggers invoke a Lambda function when objects are created, deleted, or restored; the integration type also exposes the specific S3 event type in the trigger configuration."

evidence.quote:
s3EventType:
    | 's3:ReducedRedundancyLostObject'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:*'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:Put'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:Post'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:Copy'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:CompleteMultipartUpload'
    | 's3:ObjectRemoved:*'
    | 's3:ObjectRemoved:Delete'
    | 's3:ObjectRemoved:DeleteMarkerCreated'
    | 's3:ObjectRestore:*'
    | 's3:ObjectRestore:Post'
    | 's3:ObjectRestore:Completed'
    | 's3:Replication:*'

issues.statement: "For additional control over encryption keys (e.g., AWS KMS customer-managed keys), use overrides to configure the underlying CloudFormation resource directly."
issues.reason: The source confirms Bucket only has `encryption?: boolean` for AES-256 and that resource overrides exist generally. It does not state which underlying CloudFormation logical resource or path can be overridden for KMS-managed encryption, so the recommendation is plausible but not directly grounded for Bucket.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording: "BucketProps only exposes AES-256 encryption as a boolean. If you need encryption behavior that is not exposed by BucketProps, treat it as an advanced override use case and verify the generated CloudFormation resource before changing it."

evidence.quote: Escape hatch to modify the underlying CloudFormation resources Stacktape creates.

issues.statement: "A Bucket with no configuration creates a private S3 bucket. All properties in `BucketProps` are optional — the simplest valid bucket is an empty object."
issues.reason: The source shows all `BucketProps` fields are optional and the accessibility mode defaults to private, but the provided source does not include the class constructor signature for `new Bucket({})`. The claim is likely correct, but strictly the constructor validity is not proven by the supplied `.d.ts` interface alone.
issues.suggestedFix: If constructor sources are available, verify `new Bucket({})`. Otherwise, keep the simpler factual statement: "BucketProps has no required settings, and bucket accessibility defaults to private."

positiveFindings: The draft uses `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[{ label: 'TypeScript' ... }]} />` for Stacktape configuration examples and does not include hand-authored Stacktape YAML.
`<ApiReference definitionName="BucketProps" />` matches the page resource type and includes the required `Props` suffix.
The page correctly describes `directoryUpload` mirroring behavior and warns that existing bucket contents not present locally are deleted.
The draft correctly names the supported `headersPreset` values from `SupportedHeaderPreset`.
The `connectTo` environment variable examples for a resource named `uploads` correctly use `STP_UPLOADS_NAME` and `STP_UPLOADS_ARN`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the primary BucketProps surface well: every top-level Bucket property is mentioned, the API reference uses the correct BucketProps definition name, and the Stacktape config examples use the required TypeScript CodeBlock form. I found one completeness issue around S3 event-trigger variants described on the page.

issues.statement: “S3 event triggers invoke a Lambda function directly when objects are created, deleted, or restored.”
issues.reason: The draft documents S3 event triggers but names only created, deleted, and restored event categories. The source S3Integration union also includes reduced-redundancy lost-object and replication event variants. Because this section introduces S3 event triggers as a supported workflow, the page should either acknowledge the broader event-type set or narrow the statement as examples only.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the sentence to: “S3 event triggers invoke a Lambda function directly for configured S3 event types, commonly object-created, object-removed, and object-restore events; the trigger type also supports replication-related and reduced-redundancy lost-object events through the S3 trigger configuration.” Then keep the link to /configuration/triggers/s3-events for the full trigger reference.

evidence.quote:
interface S3IntegrationProps {
  /**
   * #### The type of S3 event that will trigger the function.
   */
  s3EventType:
    | 's3:ReducedRedundancyLostObject'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:*'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:Put'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:Post'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:Copy'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:CompleteMultipartUpload'
    | 's3:ObjectRemoved:*'
    | 's3:ObjectRemoved:Delete'
    | 's3:ObjectRemoved:DeleteMarkerCreated'
    | 's3:ObjectRestore:*'
    | 's3:ObjectRestore:Post'
    | 's3:ObjectRestore:Completed'
    | 's3:Replication:*'
    | 's3:Replication:OperationFailedReplication'
    | 's3:Replication:OperationNotTracked'
    | 's3:Replication:OperationMissedThreshold'
    | 's3:Replication:OperationReplicatedAfterThreshold';

positiveFindings: All top-level BucketProps properties are covered somewhere in the draft: directoryUpload, accessibility, cors, versioning, encryption, lifecycleRules, enableEventBusNotifications, and cdn.
The lifecycleRules union variants are all named: expiration, non-current-version-expiration, class-transition, non-current-version-class-transition, and abort-incomplete-multipart-upload.
The documented access modes match the source union: private, public-read, and public-read-write.
The Stacktape configuration examples are demonstrative for their sections and use <CodeBlock intellisense> with a visible TypeScript tab rather than fenced TypeScript or hand-authored YAML.
The page includes exactly one <ApiReference definitionName="BucketProps" /> component and the definition name matches the metadata typeName.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: All technical claims are precisely grounded in the source types — every property name, mode value, lifecycle rule type, and storage class comes directly from `buckets.d.ts`, with no invented options.
The 'When NOT to use' table with four concrete alternatives and links is exemplary decision-support — exactly what a first-time reader needs before committing to this resource.

The `connectTo` section closes the loop from config to runtime: shows the Stacktape config, lists the injected env vars with correct `STP_UPLOADS_NAME`/`STP_UPLOADS_ARN` names matching the resource name used in the example, and provides a complete, copy-pasteable Lambda handler using those vars.
All five lifecycle rule types from the union are acknowledged with a comparison table before the deep-dive example — no silent omissions.
FAQ covers all three required question categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, choosing/comparison) across exactly 10 Q&A pairs, and answers are appropriately concise.
The `directoryUpload` warning callout about destructive replacement behavior prevents a common foot-gun for first-timers.
Tables are used effectively for comparisons: accessibility modes, header presets, storage classes, and lifecycle rule types — each table adds signal without padding.
The CDN section provides explicit when-to-enable, when-default-is-fine, and cost/tradeoff context as required by documentation-quality rules.
The `disableUrlNormalization` and `rewriteRoutesForSinglePageApp` properties are source-grounded and explained with practical behavior descriptions.
problems: 'ARN' is used in the `connectTo` environment variable table ('The S3 bucket ARN') without any explanation. A developer with low AWS experience will not know what an ARN is, why they need it, or when to use it versus the bucket name. This is the persona's primary concern — unexplained AWS jargon.

'Presigned URL' appears twice (once in the CORS section, once in the FAQ) with no definition. For a developer with low AWS experience, this is an opaque term. A single parenthetical ('a time-limited, signed URL that grants temporary upload access without credentials') would make it accessible.

'Multipart upload' is referenced in the FAQ without explanation ('S3 requires multipart upload — most AWS SDKs handle this automatically'). The parenthetical is partially helpful but 'multipart upload' itself is jargon.

The Basic Example section shows only `new Bucket({})` — an empty config with no practical use. For a first-timer trying to 'ship something', this leaves an immediate question: 'I created a bucket, now what?' The connectTo section addresses this later, but the gap between the basic example and a working integration requires the reader to scroll significantly.

The 'Uploading files on deploy' section discusses `directoryUpload` as its own H2 section, but for a backend storage bucket (the primary use case), this is an uncommon configuration path. A first-time reader may think file upload requires this property — but the primary upload pattern is SDK-based via `connectTo`. The section ordering subtly implies directoryUpload is the main path.

The CDN section lists several CDN capabilities ('custom domains, edge functions, caching options, price class selection, and a web application firewall') but the `<Tip>` redirecting to `HostingBucket` appears only at the end of the section, after several paragraphs. A reader who just wants a static site might read the whole section before reaching the tip that there's a simpler resource.
optionalImprovements: When 'ARN' first appears (in the connectTo env vars table), add a brief parenthetical or inline note: 'ARN (Amazon Resource Name) — a unique identifier for the bucket used in IAM policies and cross-service references. Most applications only need `STP_UPLOADS_NAME`.' This removes the single most likely point of confusion for the target persona.

Where 'presigned URL' first appears (in the CORS section), add a one-sentence definition: 'A presigned URL is a time-limited, signed link generated server-side that grants a client temporary permission to upload or download a specific S3 object without needing AWS credentials.' This makes the CORS section self-contained.

Consider reordering the top-level H2 sections so 'Connecting to a bucket' appears before 'Uploading files on deploy'. Backend access via SDK is the primary use case; directory upload is a secondary deployment-time feature. This better matches the first-timer's mental journey: 'create bucket → connect it to my function → use it'.

The basic example `new Bucket({})` could note that bucket names are auto-generated by Stacktape (no naming required) — this reassures a first-time reader that the empty config is intentional and complete, not missing a required field.

In the CDN section, move the `<Tip>` redirecting to `HostingBucket` to the top of the section (before the detailed configuration prose), not the bottom. This immediately filters out readers who want a static site and prevents them from reading irrelevant configuration details.

The FAQ answer for 'How much does S3 storage cost?' is accurate but could name the specific pricing dimensions more concretely: storage per GB/month, PUT/GET requests per 1,000, and data transfer out per GB. These are findable in AWS docs and give the low-experience reader a mental model for cost estimation without inventing specific prices.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: All Stacktape config examples correctly use `defineConfig` with class-based resources inside `<CodeBlock intellisense>`. No YAML blocks, no `StacktapeConfig` type imports, no fenced TypeScript Stacktape config — the mandatory style rules are followed cleanly.

Environment variable names in the `connectTo` section are correctly derived from the example resource name (`STP_UPLOADS_NAME`, `STP_UPLOADS_ARN`), matching the canonical `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]` pattern and the source's declared `BucketReferencableParam = 'name' | 'arn'`.

The lifecycle rules section is thorough: all five union variants from the source (`expiration`, `non-current-version-expiration`, `class-transition`, `non-current-version-class-transition`, `abort-incomplete-multipart-upload`) are listed and described, and the storage class table matches the source enum exactly.

The headersPreset table enumerates all six `SupportedHeaderPreset` values from the source type. Descriptions match the JSDoc comments. This is the kind of complete union-type coverage the style guide requires.
The `directoryUpload` warning callout correctly surfaces the delete-on-sync behavior, which is a real footgun. This is production-relevant gotcha coverage done right.
The `When NOT to use` table with concrete alternatives (HostingBucket, RelationalDatabase, EfsFilesystem) is opinionated and actionable. A busy engineer can orient themselves immediately.
The handler code example (`PutObjectCommand` via AWS SDK) is realistic and copyable. It reads `STP_UPLOADS_NAME` from `process.env`, which is exactly what an engineer would write.
One `<ApiReference definitionName="BucketProps" />` component at the bottom — correct count, correct `definitionName` matching the source type name.
problems: The FAQ creates a contradiction on encryption that a production engineer will notice: the main content presents `encryption: true` as something to deliberately enable ('Enable it with `encryption: true` to enforce and make the encryption policy visible'), while the FAQ answer states 'S3 encrypts all new objects by default using server-side encryption with S3-managed keys.' An engineer reading both will reasonably ask: 'If S3 already encrypts by default, what does setting `encryption: true` actually enforce — does it add a bucket policy rejecting unencrypted PUTs, or is it just documentation?' The page does not answer this, and the source JSDoc ('Encrypt stored objects at rest (AES-256)') is similarly silent. The current wording could cause engineers to skip the flag thinking it is redundant.

The `fileOptions` / `DirectoryUploadFilter` property is mentioned in prose ('use `fileOptions` to set custom HTTP headers and tags on files matching specific glob patterns') but no code example is provided. The `DirectoryUploadFilter` interface has `includePattern`, `excludePattern`, `headers`, and `tags` — enough structure that an engineer cannot infer the shape from prose alone. Someone trying to set `Cache-Control: max-age=31536000` on `.js`/`.css` files while keeping HTML uncached (a very common use case) gets only a sentence and must guess or look elsewhere.

The CDN decision-support section gives a vague cost signal ('typically pays for itself by reducing S3 bandwidth charges on high-traffic buckets') without any concrete guidance on what 'high-traffic' means. The style guide explicitly requires a cost or tradeoff signal concrete enough for the reader to decide. A production engineer setting this for the first time needs a rough threshold — not a tautology.

The CORS section states that `enabled: true` with no custom rules 'uses permissive defaults (`*` origins, all methods)' but then immediately shows a code example with explicit `corsRules`. The introductory sentence implies you'd set `enabled: true` alone first and then optionally add rules, but the example jumps straight to rules without showing the simpler permissive case. This ordering could confuse engineers who want the quick permissive setup for development.
mandatoryFixes: Resolve the encryption contradiction between the main content and the FAQ. The source `BucketProps.encryption` JSDoc says 'Encrypt stored objects at rest (AES-256)' without specifying whether it goes beyond S3's built-in default. Given source ambiguity, the safest fix is to write cautiously: either (a) remove the claim that S3 already encrypts by default from the FAQ, or (b) add a brief explanation of what `encryption: true` adds beyond the S3 default (e.g., 'adds an explicit bucket policy that rejects unencrypted PUT requests, making the enforcement visible and auditable in your IaC'). Do not leave both statements side-by-side without explaining the difference — a production engineer will be confused.
optionalImprovements: Add a `fileOptions` code example inside the `directoryUpload` section showing how to set `Cache-Control` headers for `.js`/`.css` files while leaving HTML uncached. The `DirectoryUploadFilter` shape (`includePattern`, `headers: [{key, value}]`) is not obvious from prose and this is a common enough use case that engineers will want it.

Strengthen the CDN decision-support paragraph with a rough threshold: for example, 'CloudFront data transfer is typically cheaper than S3 egress after ~1 GB/month, and latency improvements are meaningful for users more than ~500ms from the source region.' Even an order-of-magnitude signal is more useful than 'typically pays for itself.'

For the CORS section, show the minimal permissive case (`cors: { enabled: true }`) as a first example before showing the stricter production example with explicit `corsRules`. Two examples with a brief label each ('Development — permissive' vs. 'Production — restricted origins') would eliminate the ordering confusion.

The `accessibilityMode: 'public-read'` example sets the mode but the surrounding prose recommends `connectTo` for compute access and presigned URLs as the alternative to full public-read. A one-sentence note on when presigned URLs are a better option than `public-read` (e.g., 'for time-limited access to specific objects without exposing the whole bucket') would help engineers make the right call.

In the `connectTo` section, mention that if the bucket has a CDN (`cdn` property set), additional CDN-related referenceable params beyond `NAME` and `ARN` may be injected — or explicitly note that CDN params are not injected via `connectTo` but are accessible via `$ResourceParam`. The source `BucketReferencableParam = 'name' | 'arn' | CdnReferenceableParam` suggests CDN params exist, but `connectTo` docs only show `NAME` and `ARN`.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Self-contained, fact-dense opening paragraph (57 words) answers 'what is this and why does it matter' with a concrete durability figure and pricing model — extractable by AI engines verbatim.
All five lifecycle rule types are named and shown in a table with a working multi-rule example; no union variant is silently omitted.

Every opt-in feature (CDN, versioning, CORS, EventBridge notifications, Transfer Acceleration) gets explicit when-to-enable guidance, a when-the-default-is-fine signal, and a cost or tradeoff note — this is decision support, not just configuration docs.

The 'Connecting to a bucket' section uses the example resource name to derive concrete environment variable names (STP_UPLOADS_NAME, STP_UPLOADS_ARN) and follows with a complete, runnable AWS SDK snippet — immediately actionable.

FAQ has exactly 10 questions spanning all three required categories: Stacktape-specific ('Can I attach a custom domain'), AWS use-case ('What is the maximum object size in S3'), and comparison ('S3 vs EFS — when should I use each').
Tables are used consistently and effectively for comparisons: access modes, header presets, storage classes, and lifecycle rule types — each table is directly citeable.
All three accessibility modes (private, public-read, public-read-write) are named and shown in a single table with a clear opinionated recommendation ('Most buckets should stay private').
No marketing language, no unsupported claims — every behavioral statement is grounded in the provided type definitions.
problems: Import lines (e.g., `import { defineConfig, Bucket } from 'stacktape';`) appear ABOVE the `// [!code focus-start]` marker in every CodeBlock example, making them visible in the rendered snippet. The style guide explicitly lists this as a mandatory fix: imports must be hidden by placing them inside the focus markers or before the focus block in a way that renders them hidden.

The 'Versioning and encryption' H2 conflates two independent features — versioning (object history, cost implications, lifecycle prerequisites) and encryption (AES-256 at-rest toggle). These are different decisions with different tradeoffs. Combining them under one heading reduces scannability and citation clarity for both topics.
mandatoryFixes: Hide all `import { ... } from 'stacktape'` lines from rendered output in every CodeBlock. Place them above or inside the focus region such that `// [!code focus-start]` wraps the import along with the rest of the config body, or use a dedicated hide annotation. The style guide classes a visible Stacktape import line in a rendered snippet as a forbidden form / mandatory fix.
optionalImprovements: Split 'Versioning and encryption' into two separate H2 sections ('Versioning' and 'Encryption') to improve TOC clarity and allow each topic to be cited independently by search engines.

The CDN section mentions 'price class selection' as a supported option but doesn't name the `cloudfrontPriceClass` property or its supported values (PriceClass_100, PriceClass_200, PriceClass_All). One sentence explaining the tradeoff between coverage and cost would anchor this for readers before they follow the CDN link.

The `disableS3TransferAcceleration` prose in the 'Uploading files on deploy' section is brief. Consider adding the small surcharge context (per-GB cost) and noting that it only applies during directory-upload deploys — not to runtime S3 SDK calls from Lambda functions or containers.

The 'Basic example' section says 'All properties in BucketProps are optional — the simplest valid bucket is an empty object.' This is true but slightly surprising. A one-sentence note that this creates a private, unencrypted bucket in the deploying region would help readers understand the implicit defaults before they reach the configuration sections.

Consider a brief note in the 'Event-driven workflows' section clarifying that S3 triggers are configured on the Lambda function resource (via triggers array), not on the Bucket resource itself — this is a common point of confusion for new users navigating the config.

issues.statement: The headersPreset table describes `gatsby-static-website` as "Framework-specific caching" with best-for "Gatsby builds"
issues.reason: The source JSDoc for the `headersPreset` property only describes the behavior of `single-page-app`, `static-website`, and the astro/sveltekit/nuxt variants. The `gatsby-static-website` value exists in the `SupportedHeaderPreset` union type, so its presence is correct, but the specific behavior description ("Framework-specific caching") is not grounded in the source. The other presets' descriptions are directly sourced from the JSDoc.
issues.suggestedFix: Either describe gatsby-static-website more cautiously (e.g., "Gatsby-optimized caching") or find the implementation that defines the actual cache behavior. Since the source only lists it as a type value without description, a brief generic label is acceptable — just avoid implying you know the specific caching semantics.

evidence.quote:
type SupportedHeaderPreset =
  | 'gatsby-static-website'
  | 'static-website'
  | 'single-page-app'
  | 'astro-static-website'
  | 'sveltekit-static-website'
  | 'nuxt-static-website';

- **`single-page-app`**: HTML never cached, hashed assets cached forever. For React/Vue/Angular SPAs.
   * - **`static-website`**: CDN-cached, never browser-cached. For generic static sites.
   * - **`astro-static-website`** / **`sveltekit-static-website`** / **`nuxt-static-website`**: Framework-specific
   *   caching (hashed build assets cached forever, HTML always fresh).

issues.statement: The lifecycle rules table describes `non-current-version-expiration` as requiring `versioning: true` but does not note the same requirement for `non-current-version-class-transition`
issues.reason: Both rule types operate on non-current object versions, which only exist when versioning is enabled. The source JSDoc explicitly says "Requires `versioning: true`" for `NonCurrentVersionExpirationProps.daysAfterVersioned` but not for `NonCurrentVersionClassTransitionProps.daysAfterVersioned`. The omission in the draft table is an asymmetry that could confuse a reader who enables transition rules without versioning.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a note to the `non-current-version-class-transition` table row matching the expiration row's caveat, e.g., "Move old object versions to a cheaper storage class after a set number of days (requires `versioning: true`)".

positiveFindings: All BucketProps property names and types match the source exactly — directoryUpload, accessibility, cors, versioning, encryption, lifecycleRules, enableEventBusNotifications, cdn.
The three accessibility modes (private, public-read, public-read-write) and the default (private) are correctly sourced from BucketAccessibility.accessibilityMode.
All five lifecycle rule types match the source union exactly: expiration, non-current-version-expiration, class-transition, non-current-version-class-transition, abort-incomplete-multipart-upload.
The five storage class values (STANDARD_IA, ONEZONE_IA, INTELLIGENT_TIERING, GLACIER, DEEP_ARCHIVE) match the ClassTransitionProps.storageClass union verbatim.
CORS allowedMethods enum values (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD, *) match the source BucketCorsRule.allowedMethods type exactly.
The connectTo environment variables (STP_UPLOADS_NAME, STP_UPLOADS_ARN) correctly follow the STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM] pattern with params from BucketReferencableParam ('name' | 'arn').
The enableEventBusNotifications default (false) is correctly sourced from the @default annotation.
The disableS3TransferAcceleration default (false, meaning acceleration is enabled) is correctly stated and scoped to the directoryUpload context.

BucketCdnConfiguration correctly extends CdnConfiguration — the draft's claims about customDomains, edge functions, caching options, price class, and firewall are all verified as real properties on CdnConfiguration.
The ApiReference component uses definitionName="BucketProps" which matches the source interface name exactly.
All cross-links in the draft point to valid routes in the navigation index — no invented routes.

The S3Integration s3EventType values cited in the event-driven workflows section (object-created, object-removed, object-restore, replication, reduced-redundancy) all match the source union in events.d.ts.
Code examples use correct Stacktape class names (Bucket, LambdaFunction, StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging) and properly structured defineConfig with focus markers.

The claim that connectTo grants 'read, write, and delete object permissions' is confirmed by the source code: getStatementsForAccessingBucket grants s3:*Object*, s3:Abort*, s3:*MultipartUpload*, s3:ListBucket, and s3:GetBucket*.
verifierResults.summary: This draft is exceptionally well-grounded against the source files. Every BucketProps property, enum value, default, and type constraint was verified and found accurate. All code examples use correct class names, proper defineConfig structure, and valid property values. Cross-links all resolve to valid navigation routes. The connectTo permissions claim was verified against the actual IAM resolution code. Only two low-severity issues were found: (1) the gatsby-static-website preset description is not specifically grounded in source JSDoc (though the type value exists), and (2) a minor asymmetry where non-current-version-class-transition doesn't note the versioning requirement while non-current-version-expiration does. No high or medium severity issues were identified.

The draft is mostly grounded in the provided Bucket, connectTo, lifecycle, CORS, and S3 integration source types. I found a few corrections worth making around private access wording, the generic connectTo environment variable pattern, and unsupported naming of inherited CDN options.

issues.statement: “This creates a private bucket that only your stack's compute resources (Lambda functions, containers) can access when granted permission through connectTo.”
issues.reason: The source defines private bucket access more broadly: compute resources and any entities with sufficient IAM permissions can read/write. Saying “only your stack's compute resources” is too narrow and can mislead readers about IAM access paths outside connectTo.
issues.suggestedFix: Write: “This creates a private bucket. Stacktape compute resources can access it when you grant permissions with connectTo, and other AWS identities can access it only if they have sufficient IAM permissions.”

evidence.quote: - `private` (default): Only compute resources and entities with sufficient IAM permissions can read from or write to the bucket.

issues.statement: FAQ: “Use the AWS S3 SDK ... with the bucket name from the `STP_[BUCKET_NAME]_NAME` environment variable injected by connectTo.”
issues.reason: The documented connectTo variable naming pattern is resource-name based, not bucket-name based. The page correctly uses `STP_UPLOADS_NAME` elsewhere, but this generic pattern should be `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_NAME` to match the source and the canonical docs rule.
issues.suggestedFix: Change `STP_[BUCKET_NAME]_NAME` to `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_NAME`, and optionally add “for a resource named `uploads`, this is `STP_UPLOADS_NAME`.”

evidence.quote: Injects environment variables with connection details: `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]`
**`Bucket`** — read/write/delete objects → `NAME`, `ARN`

issues.statement: “The bucket CDN configuration also supports custom domains (via `customDomains`), edge functions, caching options, price class selection, and a web application firewall.”
issues.reason: The provided source confirms that bucket CDN config extends a shared `CdnConfiguration`, and it explicitly shows bucket-specific `rewriteRoutesForSinglePageApp` and `disableUrlNormalization`. The source bundle provided for this verification does not include the `CdnConfiguration` definition, so the named inherited fields are not grounded here.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to source-grounded bucket CDN behavior: “The bucket CDN configuration includes the shared CDN options plus bucket-specific SPA routing and URL normalization controls. See the CDN resource page for the full shared CDN surface.”

evidence.quote: interface BucketCdnConfiguration extends CdnConfiguration {
rewriteRoutesForSinglePageApp?: boolean;
disableUrlNormalization?: boolean;

positiveFindings: The page uses TypeScript `defineConfig` examples inside `<CodeBlock intellisense>` and does not hand-author YAML Stacktape config.
The API reference uses `<ApiReference definitionName="BucketProps" />`, which matches the page resource type.

Lifecycle rule types, storage class values, CORS method values, directory upload behavior, EventBridge notification default, and bucket referenceable params are accurately reflected from the provided source.
The `connectTo` concrete example uses the correct resource-derived variables: `STP_UPLOADS_NAME` and `STP_UPLOADS_ARN`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the main BucketProps surface well: all top-level BucketProps are mentioned, important union variants for lifecycle rules, accessibility, CORS methods, storage classes, and directory upload header presets are acknowledged, and the CodeBlock examples generally demonstrate the section topics. I found no API-completeness, union-coverage, or demonstrativeness issues that meet the requested threshold.
positiveFindings: All top-level BucketProps fields are covered: directoryUpload, accessibility, cors, versioning, encryption, lifecycleRules, enableEventBusNotifications, and cdn.
Lifecycle rule union variants are explicitly named: expiration, non-current-version-expiration, class-transition, non-current-version-class-transition, and abort-incomplete-multipart-upload.
Access mode variants are named and compared: private, public-read, and public-read-write.
Every Stacktape configuration example uses <CodeBlock intellisense> with a TypeScript tab, and each example sets the property discussed in its section.
The API reference uses the exact page metadata type name: <ApiReference definitionName="BucketProps" />.
